task_context.description: This is a detector design optimization task that involves creating and testing multiple configurations of a sampling electromagnetic calorimeter to determine the optimal layer thickness ratio between tungsten absorber plates and plastic scintillator active layers. The task requires systematic comparison of three specific sampling configurations with different absorber-to-scintillator thickness ratios, while maintaining constant total absorber depth. The optimization focuses on energy resolution performance through Monte Carlo simulation using Geant4, with specific emphasis on extracting the stochastic term from energy resolution measurements across multiple beam energies. The task combines detector physics design principles with computational simulation and statistical analysis to identify the best-performing sampling fraction.
task_context.goal: Determine which sampling fraction (absorber-to-scintillator thickness ratio) gives the best stochastic term in energy resolution for a tungsten-plastic scintillator electromagnetic calorimeter
constraints: Total depth target approximately 25 radiation lengths of tungsten (~8.75 cm absorber total)
Must test exactly 3 configurations with specified thickness ratios
Total absorber depth ≈ 25 X₀ in each configuration
Energy sweep limited to 1, 5, 20 GeV for tractable simulation
Use electrons (e-) in Geant4 particle gun
Absorber material fixed as tungsten (X₀ = 0.35 cm)
Active material fixed as plastic scintillator

observations.content: Task classified with 98% confidence

This workflow systematically tests three specific sampling configurations as requested, using energy sweeps for efficient simulation. Each configuration maintains ~25 radiation lengths total tungsten depth while varying the absorber-to-scintillator ratio. The analysis extracts stochastic terms by fitting energy resolution across multiple energies, enabling direct comparison to identify the optimal sampling fraction for electromagnetic calorimetry.
